one_line_takeaway: LLM 'unlearning' usually suppresses rather than deletes: task-level accuracy and MIA can look forgotten while internal representations stay intact and minimal fine-tuning revives the data, so erasure must be certified with representation-level drift metrics, not output metrics.
key_finding: Task-level metrics cannot distinguish reversible suppression from true erasure. Single-shot unlearning is essentially always reversible, non-catastrophic: activations stay intact (PCA similarity ~1.0, CKA ~1.0) and relearning restores forget accuracy (e.g. GA+KL and RLabel recover F.Acc to ~77% from Yi-6B's 78.9% original). In continual unlearning, mild hyperparameters give reversible (catastrophic or not) forgetting that relearning fully restores, whereas aggressive settings (high LR / many requests) cause irreversible catastrophic collapse of BOTH forget and retain sets. Crucially, mean PCA distance measured BEFORE relearning predicts recoverability that accuracy cannot: large pre-relearning drift predicts irreversibility. The min-k%-prob MIA AUC is shown to be an unreliable erasure metric -- it can rebound to high values after relearning even when the model's capability is permanently destroyed, because it measures probability separability, not deletion. The idealized irreversible, non-catastrophic regime was never observed in main experiments (even 10% of the forget set restored performance); only a *seemingly* irreversible, non-catastrophic case was engineered via GA+GD+WAGLE with restricted relearning data. A side finding: relearning on the forget set can exceed original accuracy, suggesting unlearning acts as an implicit contrastive regularizer.
core_contribution: The paper (ICML 2026) presents the first systematic, representation-level analysis of whether LLM machine unlearning genuinely erases information or merely suppresses it. Its own contributions are: (1) a diagnostic toolkit operating on internal activations/parameters rather than task outputs -- PCA similarity, PCA shift, centered kernel alignment (CKA), and Fisher information (FIM) -- plus a new summary scalar, the mean PCA distance, measuring layer-averaged representational drift; (2) a two-axis taxonomy of four forgetting regimes (reversible/irreversible x catastrophic/non-catastrophic); (3) a controlled 'relearning' probe (brief fine-tuning on a size-matched dataset) used as the operational test of reversibility; (4) a first-order perturbation theory linking small/localized weight edits to reversible forgetting and large/distributed edits to irreversible drift, and explaining why task-level accuracy and probability-based MIA can be misleading; and (5) identification of a case of *seemingly* irreversible, non-catastrophic forgetting via GA+GD+WAGLE. Note: the borrowed diagnostics (PCA sim/shift from Zheng et al. 2025, CKA from Kornblith et al. 2019, FIM from Kirkpatrick et al.) and the unlearning algorithms (GA/NPO/RLabel families, RMU, UnDIAL, AltPO, PDU, WAGLE) are prior work; the paper's novelty is assembling them into a reversibility-diagnosis framework and taxonomy, not inventing new unlearning methods.
threat_model: Privacy/safety threat that unlearning is claimed to mitigate but may not: LLMs memorize sensitive, copyrighted, or harmful training text, and regulations (e.g. EU Right to be Forgotten) demand removal. The paper's security claim is that current unlearning provides a false sense of erasure -- 'forgotten' knowledge can be revived by an adversary through minimal fine-tuning (even on unrelated/benign data), and prior work also shows revival via low-bit quantization, adversarial prompting/jailbreak, and in-context (few-shot) recovery. The relearning probe models a worst-case adversary who fine-tunes on a small, size-matched dataset without access to the original pre-training corpus. The paper argues task-level metrics (forget accuracy, perplexity, min-k%-prob MIA AUC) systematically overstate erasure, so a defender relying on them could ship a model whose suppressed secrets remain recoverable.
limitations: Reversibility is defined relative to a specific bounded, size-matched relearning protocol; the paper explicitly notes precise thresholds separating the four regimes are non-trivial and depend on method and task complexity, so the taxonomy is protocol-relative rather than absolute. The 'irreversible, non-catastrophic' result is only 'seemingly' irreversible under constrained relearning data (50% retain or equal-sized unrelated) -- not proven durable against a stronger adversary. The perturbation theory is a first-order intuition on a simplified feed-forward network, not a formal guarantee for real transformers. Experiments center on Yi-6B and Qwen-2.5-7B (with additional models in appendix), the 6-9B scale; larger frontier models are untested. Evaluation forget/retain data are the arXiv/GitHub/NuminaMath synthetic-task splits rather than real privacy-sensitive corpora. No new unlearning algorithm achieving genuine erasure is proposed; the work is diagnostic.

datasets_used: arXiv abstracts (simple unlearning task, from Yao et al. 2024)
GitHub code (simple unlearning task, from Yao et al. 2024)
MATH (0-shot) math utility for complex task
quantitative_results: Yi-6B original baseline: F.Acc 78.9%, R.Acc 65.5%, MIA AUC 70.9%
Single unlearning: retain accuracy drops only 2-5%; relearning recovers F.Acc to ~77% for GA+KL and RLabel (reversible, non-catastrophic)
Continual GA at LR=3e-5, N=100 (irreversible catastrophic): unlearn F.Acc 0.0% / R.Acc 0.0%; after relearn still F.Acc 2.1% / R.Acc 1.8%
Continual GA at LR=5e-6, N=100 (reversible catastrophic): unlearn F.Acc 9.1% / R.Acc 6.2%; after relearn F.Acc 80.0% / R.Acc 65.0%
Continual GA at LR=3e-6, N=100: unlearn F.Acc 16.8%; after relearn F.Acc 80.8% (full recovery)
Mean PCA distance (forget) after relearn: 104.58 at LR=3e-5 vs 1.37 at LR=5e-6 vs 2.11 at LR=3e-6 -- large residual drift only in the irreversible case
MIA AUC behaves erratically under aggressive unlearning: falls below 50% during unlearn then misleadingly rebounds after relearn despite permanent capability loss

GA+GD+WAGLE seemingly-irreversible non-catastrophic (LR=4e-5, N=50, relearn on unrelated data): after relearn F.Acc 31.5% (forget stays low), R.Acc 53.5%, forget PCA dist 24.56 vs retain PCA dist 8.12
Relearning on the forget set can exceed original accuracy (e.g. NPO+KL relearn F.Acc up to 83.8%, +4.9 over original)
baselines_compared: Task-level metrics (F.Acc, R.Acc, F.Ppl/R.Ppl, min-k%-prob MIA AUC) contrasted against the paper's representation-level toolkit (PCA similarity/shift, CKA, FIM, mean PCA distance)
Unlearning methods evaluated: GA, GA+GD, GA+KL, NPO, NPO+KL, RLabel (six canonical)
Additional unlearning paradigms: RMU, UnDIAL, AltPO, PDU, and GA+GD+WAGLE
Relearning data sources compared: forget set (worst case) vs domain-matched retain subset vs unrelated OOD data
Alternative recovery attacks that FAIL under reversible catastrophic forgetting: prompt attacks, jailbreaking, quantization, and 5-shot in-context recovery
Models: Yi-6B and Qwen-2.5-7B (main); Llama-3-8B, Llama-3-8B-Instruct, Qwen2.5-3B, Qwen3-8B-Base, Qwen3-14B (appendix)
